Conventional wisdom in the sports media business, of course, is that the NB=
A season only truly begins on Christmas Day, when ABC, ESPN, and TNT teleca=
st five nationally intriguing games from noon until past midnight, eastern =
time. That largely remained true last season despite a small bump from the =

=E2=80=99t quite move the needle, alas, with ratings down double digits.) O=
n yesterday=E2=80=99s Dan

gue when he mused: =E2=80=9CWe need to seriously consider starting at Chris=
tmas.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

But there=E2=80=99s a little more white-knuckling this year. For starters, =
the NFL=E2=80=99s own manifest destiny threatens to challenge the NBA=E2=80=
=99s unofficial debut. The Shield is famously programming two Christmas Day=
 games on Netflix, and a musical appearance from Beyonc=C3=A9 to boot. Exac=
erbating matters, of course, is the fact that ESPN executives aren=E2=80=99=
t thrilled with their early-season numbers. And while=E2=80=94yes, yes=E2=
=80=94no one in Bristol starts obsessing over basketball ratings until Chri=
stmas, the data so far is pretty brutal, especially given that Disney will =
be paying the league $2.5 billion a season for the next decade beginning ne=
xt year: Viewership for the NBA on ESPN is down 21 percent year over year. =
(TNT=E2=80=99s numbers are down 11 percent in the network=E2=80=99s final s=
eason.)

Privately, I=E2=80=99m told, ESPN executives are saying all the right thing=
s, and remain confident that the numbers will pick up after football season=
=2E But=E2=80=A6 they will be really sp=
ooked if the rebound isn=E2=80=99t com=
plete by, say, March. Meanwhile, there are any number of theories explainin=
g the decline: LeBron and Steph are too old, the game has been hijacked by =
three pointers, the NBA is too =E2=80=9Cwoke,=E2=80=9D the league=E2=80=99s=
 best young players are in tiny markets, etcetera. Commissioner Adam Silver=
 even floated his own theory this week, suggesting ( https://www.nytimes.co=

t where people are watching more programming on streaming than they are on =

eason why, for our new television deals, which will enter into next year, e=

o streaming service, putting aside how the actual game is played on the flo=
or, it=E2=80=99s going to allow us, from a production standpoint, to do all=
 kinds of things that you can=E2=80=99t do through traditional television. =
All kinds of new functionality, all kinds of new options and screens that a=

Well, It Can=E2=80=99t Get Worse
--------------------------

Silver may be the least concerned of all when it comes to the NBA=E2=80=99s=
 early-season numbers, especially considering that the league just inked ri=
ch 11-year deals with Disney, Amazon, and NBC. Silver cited social media an=
d attendance stats to demonstrate that the NBA is still popular, notwithsta=

that the NBA=E2=80=99s endless schedule=E2=80=9482 regular season games, pl=
us a multi-round playoff format=E2=80=94offers his media partners significa=
nt advertising inventory. In this increasingly fractured media ecosystem, t=

=C2=A0

Of course, grizzled sports media veterans have their own theory about the r=

nal talent, scored ESPN its largest opening night audience in 11 years. Thi=

=C2=A0

At least the NBA is not alone. The strength of the NFL (which is essentiall=

																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">I singled out =

																		<li style=3D"Margin: 0 0 24px 0;"><em>More Flagship predi=
ctions</em>: In Monday=E2=80=99s private email, I cited MoffettNathanson re=
search that predicted modest success for ESPN Flagship: 1 million paid subs=
cribers by the end of 2026, and as many as 3 million by 2030. Today, a far =
more optimistic prediction from Wells Fargo analyst <strong>Steven Cahall <=
/strong>crossed my desk. In a note, Cahall suggested that ESPN=E2=80=99s di=
rect-to-consumer service could reach as many as <em>12 million</em> subscri=
bers by 2027, and grow to 17 million by 2030. Cahall also estimated that Fl=
agship would launch at $22.99 per month, a price which would presumably hel=
p grow the subscriber base quickly. =E2=80=9CWe don=E2=80=99t think Disney =
wants to premiumize the offering,=E2=80=9D he wrote. =E2=80=9CWe think pric=
ing might have been higher if Venu were to launch, but now that=E2=80=99s u=
ncertain after the preliminary injunction.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>Perhaps most i=
mportantly, Cahall expressed confidence that Flagship could exist
alongside ESPN=E2=80=99s linear networks=E2=80=94ESPN, ESPN2, ESPN Deportes=
, ESPN News, ESPNU, SEC Network, ACC Network, etcetera=E2=80=94that make so=
 much money for Disney in the cable bundle. Cahall=E2=80=99s estimates sugg=
ested that Flagship will derive a majority of its subscribers from consumer=
s who have already ditched cable. He also hypothesized that 2 million subsc=
ribers will dump cable for Flagship by 2027, and 4 million by 2030.</li>
																		<li style=3D"Margin: 0 0 24px 0;"><em>Fox Sports=E2=80=99=
 streaming strategy</em>: Cahall also dove into Fox Sports=E2=80=99 streami=
ng plans. Fox, of course, has become the darling of Wall Street, at least a=
mong its peer set, for taking a wait-and-see approach to streaming its spor=
ts content=E2=80=94and, frankly, shorting the cable industry years ago whil=
e also keeping its powder dry in its approach to O.T.T. While CBS, Disney, =
and NBC spent boatloads of money creating their own (almost entirely unprof=
itable) streaming services, Fox has stayed on the sidelines to see how ever=
ything shakes out.<br/><br/>Cahall predicted that Fox Sports would unveil i=
ts sports streaming strategy within the next six months, but he doesn=E2=80=
=99t expect it to follow the lead set by ESPN. Rather, Cahall expects Fox S=
ports to cut deals with existing providers, like allowing Prime to offer a =
subscription to all of Fox Sports=E2=80=99 content. Or, perhaps the company=
 could work with YouTube TV on a package similar
to the streamer=E2=80=99s NFL Sunday Ticket<em> </em>deal, whereby it sells=
 subscriptions to Fox=E2=80=99s portfolio of games. =E2=80=9CRoku and Apple=
 TV+ are also neutral streaming distributors,=E2=80=9D Cahall wrote. =E2=80=
=9CEventually, we think ESPN Flagship could include a branded Fox tile, sim=
ilar to how ESPN exists on Disney+ today.=E2=80=9D Cahall also estimated th=
at such a Fox Sports-branded offering would cost around $10 per month. =E2=
=80=9CWe see millions of potential incremental subs at high ARPU/margins an=
d limited impact on the bundle at this price.=E2=80=9D</li>

udos to my former <em>Sports Business Journal</em> colleague <strong>Josh C=
arpenter</strong>, who was <a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpb=

a63cece91f727fbfb3271571f5f91e69d55838" style=3D"text-decoration: underline=

t two years and involve a modest rights fee. Fox will carry LIV events on i=
ts broadcast network and cable channels, while LIV will produce the events,=
 taking a big expense off the network=E2=80=99s plate. I expect the deal wi=
ll include some sort of commitment for LIV sponsors to buy ad time during t=
he telecasts=E2=80=94a common practice known as =E2=80=9Cunderpinning.=E2=
=80=9D<br/><br/>Back in January of 2023, the CW
signed a deal to carry LIV events as a way to build out its sports portfoli=
o. The network has since signed deals to carry college football and basketb=

YTQyYWJkMTJhIiwiaW50ZXJuYWwiOiJmNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJsaW5rX2lkIjoyMDI=
zNzYxfQ/8e721c0f9f793a9f1576ad8e2e00c5e8171b8b7e0b54226511ae877d0ed85801" s=
tyle=3D"text-decoration: underline; color: #d40000;">minority stake</a> in =
PGA Tour Enterprises for $1.5 billion. Monahan didn=E2=80=99t get into the =
specifics of the deal=E2=80=94it=E2=80=99s currently before the Justice Dep=
artment and is expected to be fast-tracked once Trump takes over=E2=80=94bu=
t he did talk about renewed momentum for a tour that=E2=80=99s been reeling=
 since the fall of 2021, when LIV Golf launched and began poaching some of =
the tour=E2=80=99s biggest names, like <strong>Bryson DeChambeau
</strong>and <strong>Brooks Koepka</strong>. Back then, of course, the PGA =
tried to retain talent amid accusations of sportswashing against LIV and th=
e Saudis. Obviously, that defense is out the window now.<br/><br/>If the de=
al gets D.O.J. approval, it could be finalized as soon as this spring. On t=
hat timetable, fans should expect LIV golfers and PGA Tour players to start=
 competing against each other again by 2026, which would really validate th=
e risk that the DeChambeaus of the world took. They got their enormous chec=
ks, and now they=E2=80=99ll be reunited with their old buddies from the tou=
r.<br/><br/>Meanwhile, the PGA Tour has made several fan-friendly changes, =
such as reducing the number of golfers competing in any given event, which =
essentially ensures that its tournaments will feature known golfers. It=E2=
=80=99s also putting the finishing touches on PGA Tour Studios, next to its=
 Ponte Vedra headquarters. The tour produces events for ESPN+ and others fr=
om those studios. One potential
outcome: It could look to=C2=A0 develop its production chops to the point t=
hat streamers will bid on its rights when they are up again, with the tour =
handling its own production. But that would be a much longer-term goal, giv=
en that CBS Sports and NBC Sports hold those rights until 2030.</li>
																		<li aria-level=3D"1" style=3D"Margin: 0 0 24px 0;"><em>Ne=
w job alert</em>: Another big topic at the town hall was the PGA Tour=E2=80=
=99s decision to bring on a C.E.O. who would work with Monahan and report t=
o the tour=E2=80=99s policy board and board of directors. Falcons owner <st=
rong>Arthur</strong> <strong>Blank</strong>, who is on the PGA Tour Enterpr=
ises board, will head up the search committee. Other members of the committ=
ee include <strong>Tiger</strong> <strong>Woods</strong>, <strong>Adam</str=
ong> <strong>Scott</strong>, <strong>Sam</strong> <strong>Kennedy</strong>,=
 and <strong>Joe</strong> <strong>Gorder</strong>. The Tour also hired the =
magnificently named consultancy Korn<strong> </strong>Ferry to identify can=
didates.<br/><br/>Of course, Monahan told staffers that he wanted to hire a=
 C.E.O. <em>11 months ago</em>, when the tour launched PGA Tour Enterprises=
 and brought on Strategic Sports Group as equity investors to the tune of $=
1.5 billion. PGA Tour Enterprises
essentially is the tour=E2=80=99s for-profit arm, combining the tour=E2=80=
=99s commercial businesses and rights with the DP World Tour. The C.E.O. wi=
ll be responsible for growing the business and integrating P.I.F.=E2=80=99s=
 incoming $1.5 billion investment.</li>

=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"m=

x;line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;">
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">Every fall, wh=
en ESPN executives map out their expectations for autumnal viewership, thei=
r conversations <em>naturally </em>focus on the NFL and college football. A=
fter all, ESPN holds the coveted rights to <em>Monday Night Football </em>a=
nd has invested nine figures in the SEC, ACC, and Big 12. NBA ratings=E2=80=
=94despite ESPN=E2=80=99s recently extended, multibillion-dollar deal with =
the league=E2=80=94are a mere afterthought as the leaves change colors.<br/=
><br/>Conventional wisdom in the sports media business, of course, is that =
the NBA season only <em>truly </em>begins on Christmas Day, when ABC, ESPN,=
 and TNT telecast five nationally intriguing games from noon until past mid=
night, eastern time. That largely remained true last season despite a small=
 bump from the inaugural NBA Cup. (This season=E2=80=99s newly renamed Emir=
ates Cup didn=E2=80=99t quite <em>move the needle</em>, alas, with ratings =
down double digits.) On yesterday=E2=80=99s <em>Dan
Patrick Show</em>, <strong>Charles Barkley</strong> no doubt spoke for many=
 in and around the league when he mused: =E2=80=9CWe need to seriously cons=
ider <em>starting</em> at Christmas.=E2=80=9D</p>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">=C2=A0</p>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">But there=E2=
=80=99s a little more white-knuckling this year. For starters, the NFL=E2=
=80=99s own manifest destiny threatens to challenge the NBA=E2=80=99s unoff=
icial debut. The Shield is famously programming two Christmas Day games on =
Netflix, and a musical appearance from <strong>Beyonc=C3=A9 </strong>to boo=
t. Exacerbating matters, of course, is the fact that ESPN executives aren=
=E2=80=99t thrilled with their early-season numbers. And while=E2=80=94yes,=
 <em>yes=E2=80=94</em>no one in Bristol starts obsessing over basketball ra=
tings until Christmas, the data so far is pretty brutal, especially given t=
hat Disney will be paying the league $2.5 billion a season for the next dec=
ade beginning next year: Viewership for the NBA on ESPN is down 21 percent =
year over year. (TNT=E2=80=99s numbers are down 11 percent in the network=
=E2=80=99s final season.)</p>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0;"><br/>Privately, I=E2=80=99m told, =
ESPN executives are saying all the right things, and remain confident that =
the numbers will pick up after football season. But=E2=80=A6 they will be <=
em>really </em>spooked if the rebound isn=E2=80=99t complete by, say, March=
=2E Meanwhile, there are any number of=20=
theories explaining the decline: <stro=
ng>LeBron </strong>and <strong>Steph </strong>are too old, the game has bee=
n hijacked by three pointers, the NBA is too =E2=80=9Cwoke,=E2=80=9D the le=
ague=E2=80=99s best young players are in tiny markets, etcetera. Commission=
er <strong>Adam Silver</strong> even floated his own theory this week, <a h=
ref=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJL=
Z0dUNGVhTHZUSDQ1R0N4V3pldmlrdz0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20=
vYXRobGV0aWMvNjAwMjYwMy8yMDI0LzEyLzE3L2FkYW0tc2lsdmVyLW5iYS10di1yYXRpbmdzLz=
91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249VGhlK1ZhcnNpdHkrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxMiUyRjE5JTJGMjQlM=
jlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1UaGUrVmFyc2l0eSstK1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUyODEyJTJGMTkl=
MkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0aW9uXHUwMDI2dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1jdXN=
0b21lci5pb1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImludGVybmFsIjoiZj=
ZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6MjAyMzc2M30/9a4f89df64bc07169c51d3155=
019912b75899d592b1052051052c6b430bc50a4" style=3D"text-decoration: underlin=
e; color: #d40000;">suggesting</a> to a scrum of reporters at the Emirates =
Cup that the contracting cable
industry was to blame. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re almost at the inflection poin=
t where people are watching more programming on streaming than they are on =
traditional television,=E2=80=9D Silver said. =E2=80=9CAnd it=E2=80=99s a r=
eason why, for our new television deals, which will enter into next year, e=
very game is going to be available on a streaming service. And as we move t=
o streaming service, putting aside how the actual game is played on the flo=
or, it=E2=80=99s going to allow us, from a production standpoint, to do all=
 kinds of things that you can=E2=80=99t do through traditional television. =
All kinds of new functionality, all kinds of new options and screens that a=
re available.=E2=80=9D</p>

																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">Silver may be =
the least concerned of all when it comes to the NBA=E2=80=99s early-season =
numbers, especially considering that the league just inked rich 11-year dea=
ls with Disney, Amazon, and NBC. Silver cited social media and attendance s=
tats to demonstrate that the NBA is still popular, notwithstanding the earl=
y-season lag in viewership. Also, he knows as well as anyone that the NBA=
=E2=80=99s endless schedule=E2=80=9482 regular season games, plus a multi-r=
ound playoff format=E2=80=94offers his media partners significant advertisi=
ng inventory. In this increasingly fractured media ecosystem, that inventor=
y is arguably more valuable than the league=E2=80=99s draw as a subscriptio=
n-generator for Peacock, Disney+, Flagship, or Prime.</p>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">=C2=A0</p>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">Of course, gri=
zzled sports media veterans have their <em>own</em> theory about the rating=
s decline. According to this slightly self-serving hypothesis, last year=E2=
=80=99s early numbers were unfairly inflated due to rubbernecking interest =
in French rookie wunderkind <strong>Victor Wembanyama</strong>. Wemby, a tr=
ue generational talent, scored ESPN its largest opening night audience in 1=
1 years. This year, ESPN=E2=80=99s opening games featured a bevy of injured=
 stars and year-over-year viewership dropped more than 40 percent.=C2=A0</p=
>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0; margin-bottom: 0px;">=C2=A0</p>
																	<p style=3D"margin: 0;">At least the NBA is not alone. The=
 strength of the NFL (which is essentially flat in terms of viewership this=
 year) and college football has taken a lot of the oxygen away from other s=
ports, as well: Along with the NBA, college basketball (both men=E2=80=99s =
and women=E2=80=99s) and the NHL are down double digits.</p>
